questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant is admitted with RSV bronchiolitis. SpO2 is 91% on room air, RR 58, mild intercostal retractions, poor feeding. The physician orders albuterol. Which nursing action is most appropriate?

options.text: Administer albuterol as ordered; it is the standard of care for RSV bronchiolitis
Question the order; bronchodilators are not recommended as routine treatment for bronchiolitis — prioritize nasal suctioning and supportive care
Hold the albuterol and administer corticosteroids instead
Administer albuterol only if SpO2 drops below 88%

questions.rationale: RSV bronchiolitis is managed with supportive care only per AAP guidelines: nasal suctioning (most effective intervention for symptom relief), adequate hydration (NG feeds if needed), and supplemental O2 for SpO2 <90–92%. Bronchodilators (albuterol) and corticosteroids are NOT recommended because bronchiolitis involves viral-induced inflammation and mucus plugging — not bronchospasm — making bronchodilators ineffective. Multiple RCTs have shown no benefit. The nurse should question the order and clarify before administration. Contact precautions (RSV is spread via droplet/contact) are required.

distractorRationales.a: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Administer albuterol as ordered; it is the standard of care for RSV bronchiolitis" moves care toward routine care before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.c: Because o2 if SpO2 <90–92% suggests oxygenation or ventilation, "Hold the albuterol and administer corticosteroids instead" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Question the order; bronchodilators are not recommended as routine treatment for bronchiolitis — prioritize nasal suctioning" fits the safer response to a 6-month-old infant is admitted with RSV bronchiolitis.

questions.takeaway: RSV bronchiolitis = supportive care: nasal suction > bronchodilators; albuterol NOT routine treatment (no bronchospasm mechanism); contact precautions; O2 if SpO2 <90–92%.

visualRationale.title: Pediatric Nursing
visualRationale.caption: RSV bronchiolitis = supportive care: nasal suction > bronchodilators; albuterol NOT routine treatment (no bronchospasm mechanism); contact precautions; O2 if SpO2 <90–92%.
visualRationale.conclusion: RSV bronchiolitis = supportive care: nasal suction > bronchodilators; albuterol NOT routine treatment (no bronchospasm mechanism); contact precautions; O2 if SpO2 <90–92%.

questions.stem: A patient is scheduled for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy at 8:00 AM. Per current ASA NPO guidelines, what are the correct restrictions for solid food and clear liquids?

options.text: Nothing by mouth after midnight — no exceptions for any type of food or liquid
Solid food stopped by midnight; clear liquids (including milk) allowed until 4 hours before surgery
Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water, clear juice, black coffee) allowed until 2 hours before
No restrictions apply — laparoscopic procedures do not carry aspiration risk

questions.rationale: Current ASA NPO guidelines (2017, updated 2023): Solid foods, fried/fatty meals: NPO ≥8 hours. Light meals (toast, clear liquids): NPO ≥6 hours. Non-clear liquids including milk and formula: NPO ≥6 hours. Clear liquids (water, apple juice without pulp, clear carbonated beverages, black coffee or tea without cream, sports drinks): may be consumed up to 2 hours before elective procedures. Chewing gum and hard candy: treat as clear liquid — stop 2 hours before. These evidence-based guidelines replaced the outdated 'NPO after midnight' rule. The old rule increased patient discomfort, insulin resistance, and perioperative complications without reducing aspiration risk for clear fluids. High-risk patients (morbid obesity, GERD, pregnancy, gastroparesis, bowel obstruction) require individualized assessment and may need longer restriction.

distractorRationales.a: "Nothing by mouth after midnight — no exceptions for any type of food or liquid" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "High-risk patients (morbid obesity, GERD, pregnancy, gastroparesis, bowel obstruction) require individualized assessment and may need longer restriction" raises concern for unstable glucose physiology, so glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.b: Because high-risk patients (morbid obesity, GERD, pregnancy, gastroparesis, bowel obstruction) require individualized assessment and may need longer restriction suggests unstable glucose physiology, "Solid food stopped by midnight; clear liquids (including milk) allowed until 4 hours before surgery" would not correct the active problem. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.
distractorRationales.d: Because high-risk patients (morbid obesity, GERD, pregnancy, gastroparesis, bowel obstruction) require individualized assessment and may need longer restriction suggests unstable glucose physiology, "No restrictions apply — laparoscopic procedures do not carry aspiration risk" would not correct the active problem. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.

questions.takeaway: ASA NPO: solids ≥6-8 hrs before; clear liquids until 2 hrs before. 'NPO after midnight' is outdated and harmful. Clear liquids: water, clear juice (no pulp), black coffee/tea.

questions.stem: A nurse is preparing to administer insulin glargine (Lantus) and insulin aspart (NovoLog) to a patient with type 1 diabetes before breakfast. Which action by the nurse requires IMMEDIATE correction?

options.text: Drawing up each insulin in a separate syringe
Mixing glargine and aspart in the same syringe before administration
Injecting the aspart subcutaneously 15 minutes before the meal
Rotating injection sites between the abdomen and thigh

questions.rationale: Insulin glargine (Lantus) is a long-acting basal insulin with a specific pH formulation. It should NEVER be mixed with any other insulin, including rapid-acting insulins. Mixing glargine with other insulins alters its pH, causing it to crystallize and unpredictably changes both insulins' absorption profiles. The result is an unreliable pharmacokinetic profile that can cause severe hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia. Each insulin must be drawn in a separate syringe and administered as separate injections. Drawing up separately is correct. Injecting aspart before meals (pre-prandial) is appropriate. Site rotation is correct practice.

distractorRationales.a: The option can leave unstable glucose physiology untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Mixing glargine and aspart in the same syringe before administration" fits the safer response to the result is an unreliable pharmacokinetic profile that can cause severe hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia.
distractorRationales.c: "Injecting the aspart subcutaneously 15 minutes before the meal" misses the bedside risk because a nurse is preparing to administer insulin glargine (Lantus) and insulin aspart (NovoLog) to a patient with type points to unstable glucose physiology. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization. The stem cue "It has its own pH and formulation" makes unstable glucose physiology the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Glargine (Lantus) = NEVER MIX with other insulins. It has its own pH and formulation. Mix-up can cause crystal formation and unpredictable glycemic effect. Use separate syringes, separate injections. Applies also to detemir (Levemir).

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for four patients on a medical-surgical unit. Which patient should the nurse assess first?

options.text: A 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes who reports feeling 'shaky' and has a blood glucose of 62 mg/dL
A 44-year-old post-appendectomy (day 2) requesting pain medication, pain rated 5/10
A 71-year-old with heart failure who gained 1 lb overnight and is on daily weights
A 33-year-old with a UTI who asks when the next dose of antibiotics is due

questions.rationale: The patient with a blood glucose of 62 mg/dL has symptomatic hypoglycemia ('shaky'), which is an acute, life-threatening condition requiring immediate intervention. Untreated hypoglycemia can rapidly progress to seizures, loss of consciousness, and brain injury. The other patients represent important but lower-acuity concerns: the post-op patient has manageable pain, the heart failure patient's weight gain needs assessment but is not immediately life-threatening, and the UTI patient's antibiotic timing is a scheduled task.

distractorRationales.b: Because the patient with a blood glucose of 62 mg/dL has symptomatic hypoglycemia ('shaky'), which is an acute, life-threatening suggests unstable glucose physiology, "A 44-year-old post-appendectomy (day 2) requesting pain medication, pain rated 5/10" would not correct the active problem. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.
distractorRationales.c: Because untreated hypoglycemia can rapidly progress to seizures, loss of consciousness suggests unstable glucose physiology, "A 71-year-old with heart failure who gained 1 lb overnight and is on daily weights" would not correct the active problem. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.
distractorRationales.d: "A 33-year-old with a UTI who asks when the next dose of antibiotics is due" misses the bedside risk because apply the ABCs and Maslow's hierarchy: acute metabolic emergencies (hypoglycemia, airway compromise) always outrank scheduled pain requests, routine points to unstable glucose physiology. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.

questions.takeaway: Apply the ABCs and Maslow's hierarchy: acute metabolic emergencies (hypoglycemia, airway compromise) always outrank scheduled pain requests, routine monitoring, and informational questions.

questions.stem: A community health nurse is teaching a group of nursing students about levels of prevention. Which example correctly illustrates primary prevention?

options.text: Providing mammography screening to detect early breast cancer in women over 40
Administering the influenza vaccine to a healthy adult before flu season
Prescribing physical therapy for a patient recovering from a hip fracture
Conducting a colonoscopy to identify and remove pre-cancerous polyps

questions.rationale: Primary prevention: actions taken before disease occurs to prevent onset. Examples: immunization, health education, smoking cessation counseling, fluoridated water, condom use. Secondary prevention: early detection of existing disease (screening) — mammography, colonoscopy, blood pressure screening, Pap smear. Tertiary prevention: reducing disability and restoring function in patients with established disease — rehabilitation, cardiac rehab, chronic disease management. Mammography and colonoscopy are secondary prevention (detecting disease before symptoms). Physical therapy post-fracture is tertiary prevention.

distractorRationales.a: "Providing mammography screening to detect early breast cancer in women over 40" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Physical therapy post-fracture is tertiary prevention" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: "Prescribing physical therapy for a patient recovering from a hip fracture" misses the bedside risk because tertiary prevention: reducing disability and restoring function in patients with established disease — rehabilitation, cardiac rehab, chronic disease points to circulation or perfusion. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "Conducting a colonoscopy to identify and remove pre-cancerous polyps" moves care toward routine care before circulation and perfusion support.

questions.takeaway: Primary prevention = before disease (vaccines, education); Secondary = early detection (screening); Tertiary = limit disability/restore function (rehab). Not to be confused with clinical levels of care.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse has four patients to assess. Which patient should the nurse see FIRST, applying Maslow's hierarchy of needs?

options.text: A patient who is anxious about an upcoming procedure
A patient with a respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min and SpO2 of 88%
A patient who needs discharge teaching before going home
A patient asking for pain medication rated 4/10

questions.rationale: Maslow's hierarchy places physiological needs at the foundation. Oxygenation is the highest physiological priority. A patient with RR 8 and SpO2 88% is in respiratory distress — an immediate life-threatening concern that takes absolute priority. Anxiety (A) is a safety/psychological need. Discharge teaching (C) is a self-actualization/cognitive need. Pain rated 4/10 (D) is physiological but not immediately life-threatening compared to respiratory failure.

distractorRationales.a: "A patient who is anxious about an upcoming procedure" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "A patient with RR 8 and SpO2 88% is in respiratory distress — an immediate life-threatening concern" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: "A patient who needs discharge teaching before going home" misses the bedside risk because a patient with RR 8 and SpO2 88% is in respiratory distress — an immediate life-threatening concern points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.d: Because maslow hierarchy on NCLEX: airway/breathing/circulation (physiological) always come before safety, love/belonging, esteem suggests oxygenation or ventilation, "A patient asking for pain medication rated 4/10" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: Maslow hierarchy on NCLEX: airway/breathing/circulation (physiological) always come before safety, love/belonging, esteem, and self-actualization.

questions.stem: A nurse is completing an admission assessment on a 79-year-old patient transferred from a nursing facility. Which combination of factors identifies the HIGHEST fall risk?

options.text: Age >65 and female gender
History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic drop >20 mmHg on standing)
Mild bilateral hearing loss and BMI of 29
Mild arthritis in both knees and occasional evening confusion

questions.rationale: The highest fall risk constellation combines: (1) Prior fall history — the single strongest predictor of future falls in the elderly; (2) Polypharmacy with antihypertensives — alpha-blockers, diuretics, and calcium channel blockers commonly cause orthostatic hypotension and syncope; (3) Documented orthostatic hypotension — directly causes dizziness and falls upon position change. Both the Morse Fall Scale and STRATIFY risk tool weight prior falls most heavily. Key interventions: bed in lowest position, non-skid footwear, call light within reach, scheduled toileting (most falls occur during nighttime toileting), orthostatic vital signs before ambulation, and urgent medication review. Age and gender alone are poor predictors without functional factors.

distractorRationales.a: "Age >65 and female gender" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "The highest fall risk constellation combines: (1) Prior fall history — the single strongest predictor of future falls" raises concern for medication toxicity, so medication-safety intervention is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: "Mild bilateral hearing loss and BMI of 29" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "The highest fall risk constellation combines: (1) Prior fall history — the single strongest predictor of future falls" raises concern for medication toxicity, so medication-safety intervention is needed before routine observation.
distractorRationales.d: "Mild arthritis in both knees and occasional evening confusion" normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. The cue "The highest fall risk constellation combines: (1) Prior fall history — the single strongest predictor of future falls" raises concern for medication toxicity, so medication-safety intervention is needed before reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension. Prior fall is the strongest single predictor. Target modifiable risk factors aggressively.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: An RN is caring for four patients and needs to delegate tasks. Which task is appropriate to assign to an unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP/CNA)?

options.text: Assess the breath sounds of a patient with new-onset dyspnea
Administer a tube feeding to a stable patient via an established G-tube
Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery
Teach a diabetic patient how to perform foot care

questions.rationale: UAPs can perform tasks that are routine, repetitive, and do not require nursing judgment or assessment. Ambulating a stable postoperative patient 24 hours post-hip replacement is a safe, predictable task for UAP after the RN has assessed the patient's stability. Assessment of breath sounds (A) requires nursing judgment and cannot be delegated. Administering tube feedings (B) — in many states, enteral feeding via established G-tube can be delegated to trained UAPs, but the skill level required is greater than ambulation; this varies by state — ambulation is universally appropriate for CNA delegation. Patient education (D) is an RN responsibility requiring professional judgment.

distractorRationales.a: "Assess the breath sounds of a patient with new-onset dyspnea" moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. The cue "Ambulating a stable postoperative patient 24 hours post-hip replacement is a safe, predictable task for UAP after the" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching.
distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward premature activity instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "Assessment of breath sounds (A) requires nursing judgment and cannot be delegated" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: Because assessment of breath sounds (A) requires nursing judgment and cannot be delegated suggests oxygenation or ventilation, "Teach a diabetic patient how to perform foot care" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: UAPs can perform routine, non-assessment tasks: ambulation, ADLs, vital signs on stable patients, I&O — assessment, teaching, and medication administration remain with the RN.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A patient delivers vaginally and 30 minutes later has an estimated blood loss of 750 mL with a soft, boggy uterus displaced to the right. BP is 94/58, HR 118. Which sequence of interventions is correct?

options.text: IV oxytocin infusion, bimanual uterine compression, empty the bladder, call the provider
Empty the bladder, fundal massage, IV oxytocin infusion, call the provider if no improvement
Call the provider, obtain type and crossmatch, begin blood transfusion
Place in Trendelenburg, administer IV epinephrine, call for hysterectomy consent

questions.rationale: Postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) from uterine atony management: (1) Massage the fundus — stimulates uterine contraction. (2) Empty the bladder — a full bladder displaces the uterus and prevents contraction (uterus displaced to the right = full bladder). (3) IV oxytocin — first-line uterotonic. (4) Notify provider if no response. Bladder emptying is critical and often overlooked — the displaced, boggy uterus signals bladder distension. Epinephrine (D) and hysterectomy are not indicated at this stage.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward incomplete escalation instead of bleeding-risk intervention. The stem cue "(3) IV oxytocin — first-line uterotonic" makes bleeding or anticoagulation injury the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave bleeding or anticoagulation injury untreated because it relies on notification without the bedside safety step. "Empty the bladder, fundal massage, IV oxytocin infusion, call the provider if no improvement" fits the safer response to (3) IV oxytocin — first-line uterotonic.
distractorRationales.d: "Place in Trendelenburg, administer IV epinephrine, call for hysterectomy consent" relies on notification without the bedside safety step. The cue "BP is 94/58, HR 118" raises concern for bleeding or anticoagulation injury, so bleeding-risk intervention is needed before waiting for a callback.

questions.takeaway: Boggy uterus displaced to the right after delivery = full bladder; empty bladder first, then fundal massage + oxytocin.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A post-operative patient on day 2 following abdominal surgery complains of left calf pain, warmth, and swelling. Vital signs are stable. Which action is the NURSE'S PRIORITY?

options.text: Elevate the leg and apply warm compresses
Perform Homan's sign assessment and document the result
Notify the provider and anticipate orders for duplex ultrasound
Administer the scheduled enoxaparin and continue monitoring

questions.rationale: Unilateral calf pain, warmth, and swelling in a post-operative patient are classic signs of deep vein thrombosis (DVT). The nurse's priority is to notify the provider so imaging (duplex ultrasound — gold standard for DVT) can be ordered promptly. Homan's sign (B) is unreliable and has been removed from clinical guidelines. Warmth and elevation (A) delay diagnosis. Administering the next prophylactic dose (D) without a confirmed DVT diagnosis and provider notification is inappropriate.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "Unilateral calf pain, warmth" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.b: "Perform Homan's sign assessment and document the result" misses the bedside risk because administering the next prophylactic dose (D) without a confirmed DVT diagnosis and provider notification is inappropriate points to circulation or perfusion. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.d: "Administer the scheduled enoxaparin and continue monitoring" misses the bedside risk because the nurse's priority is to notify the provider so imaging (duplex ultrasound — gold standard for DVT) can points to circulation or perfusion. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.

questions.takeaway: Post-op calf pain + swelling = suspect DVT; notify provider and anticipate duplex ultrasound — Homan's sign is unreliable.
